text: Shannon channel capacity is C = B log2(1 + SNR) with bandwidth in hertz and SNR as a linear power ratio.
For Shannon capacity, an SNR value stated in dB can be inserted directly into log2(1+SNR).
Spectral efficiency equals throughput in bits per second divided by bandwidth in hertz.
Spectral efficiency uses throughput in bps divided by bandwidth left numerically in MHz.
Free-space path loss is 20 log10 distance_km plus 20 log10 frequency_MHz plus 32.44 dB.
The 32.44 free-space path-loss constant is used with frequency in GHz.
SINR is signal power divided by the sum of interference power and noise power in compatible linear units.
When interference is present, SINR can be calculated by dropping interference and using signal divided by noise.
Rayleigh fading outage probability is one minus exp of negative threshold SNR divided by average SNR.
Rayleigh outage probability always equals threshold SNR divided by average SNR without approximation limits.
Coherent BPSK BER in AWGN is Q of sqrt two times Eb over N0, with Eb/N0 converted to linear scale.
The BPSK Q function accepts an Eb/N0 dB value directly without conversion.
The Nyquist symbol-rate limit of an ideal noiseless baseband channel is two times bandwidth in hertz.
A bandwidth of 3 kHz must be normalized to 300 Hz before applying the Nyquist symbol-rate formula.
Under the total SNR convention, MIMO capacity is log2 det of I plus rho divided by Nt times H H Hermitian.
Total rho in the MIMO capacity expression must never be divided by the number of transmit antennas.
Friis received power uses Pt Gt Gr times the squared factor lambda divided by four pi distance.
Friis received power applies the lambda over four pi distance factor only once, without squaring.
Convert an SNR power ratio from dB to linear with ten raised to SNR_dB divided by ten.
Convert an SNR power ratio from dB to linear by dividing the dB value by twenty in the exponent.
